ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI for chicken katsu bowl as a composite dish; closest match is chicken cutlet food product (FOODON_00002764). Bowl/rice composite aspect not captured.
ontology.foodex2_description: Chicken meat, coated and fried, with rice — composite dish approximation under FoodEx2 poultry preparations
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for chicken katsu bowl. A0CKN (coated poultry) used as closest defensible anchor; rice and sauce components not individually coded.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept for chicken katsu bowl composite dish identified in clinical terminology.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 400 g, comprising a base of pearl rice (~150 g), two to three pieces of panko-breaded deep-fried chicken (~120 g), shredded cabbage slaw (~60 g), Japanese curry sauce (~50 g), goma dressing (~10 g), and one onsen egg (~50 g).

nutrient_source.title: Chicken Katsu Bowl — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Chicken Katsu Bowl (cr_id G00010540). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken from this canonical HPB record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated at 0.9 g/100g based on USDA FoodData Central data for panko-breaded fried chicken (USDA SR Legacy #05062) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this entry.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Chicken Katsu Bowl as a composite dish. GI estimated at 65 based on the dominant carbohydrate component: white jasmine/pearl rice, which has a published GI of approximately 64–72 (mean ~68) in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. The panko breadcrumb coating contributes additional refined carbohydrate but is a minor fraction of total carbohydrate. A blended GI of 65 is used as a conservative medium-GI estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (65) × carbohydrate per serving (61.6 g from 400 g × 15.4 g/100g) / 100 = 40.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 354 mg/100g; a 400 g serving delivers approximately 1,416 mg sodium — over half the WHO daily limit of 2,000 mg — with limited potassium offset at 91 mg/100g.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request katsu sauce and goma dressing on the side and use sparingly, as these are the primary sodium contributors; increasing the shredded cabbage portion improves the potassium-to-sodium ratio.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white pearl rice base (GI ~65–68) provides 15.4 g carbohydrate/100g with only 1.0 g dietary fibre/100g; a standard 400 g serving delivers approximately 61.6 g total carbohydrate with a glycemic load of ~40, which is high.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should substitute white pearl rice with brown rice or cauliflower rice to reduce glycaemic impact, request grilled or baked chicken to eliminate the panko coating carbohydrate contribution, and increase the cabbage slaw portion to improve fibre intake and slow glucose absorption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying in oil raises total fat to 3.86 g/100g with an estimated saturated fat of 0.9 g/100g; sodium at 354 mg/100g is a secondary cardiovascular risk factor; cholesterol at 18 mg/100g from the onsen egg is modest.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should request baked or grilled chicken to reduce total and saturated fat from deep-frying, limit katsu sauce to reduce sodium load, and increase the cabbage component for fibre and phytonutrient benefit.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that directly promote joint inflammation; this is a hard disqualifier for RA regardless of the moderate saturated fat content.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should request grilled or baked chicken katsu to eliminate AGE formation from deep-frying, and increase the shredded cabbage portion for sulforaphane anti-inflammatory benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Katsu Bowl is a Japanese-inspired rice bowl dish popular in Singapore's casual dining and food court scene. It consists of a base of steamed pearl (Japanese short-grain) white rice topped with one or more pieces of panko-breaded deep-fried chicken cutlet (katsu), shredded cabbage slaw dressed with goma (sesame) dressing, a ladle of Japanese-style curry sauce, and a soft-cooked onsen egg. The chicken is typically breast or thigh meat, pounded thin, coated in flour, egg wash, and coarse panko breadcrumbs, then deep-fried until golden and crispy. The onsen egg is slow-cooked at approximately 63–65°C to produce a custard-like white and a runny, jammy yolk. The flavour profile is savoury, mildly spiced from the curry, rich from the sesame dressing and egg yolk, and texturally contrasting between the crispy chicken and soft rice. Per 100 g, the dish provides 126 kcal, 7.04 g protein, 3.86 g fat, 15.4 g carbohydrate, 1.0 g dietary fibre, and 354 mg sodium. A standard 400 g serving delivers approximately 504 kcal and 61.6 g carbohydrate with a glycemic load of ~40, making it a high-GL meal. Clinically, the dish is high concern for type 2 diabetes due to the high-GI white rice base and low fibre content, moderate concern for hypertension due to sodium from katsu sauce and soy-based dressings, and high concern for rheumatoid arthritis due to AGE generation from deep-frying. It is suitable for cardiovascular disease with modifications. The dish is not vegetarian, not vegan, and contains gluten (panko), egg, sesame, and soy allergens.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry for Chicken Katsu Bowl (cr_id G00010540) with input confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy closest match for panko-breaded fried chicken. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous white rice data. All mandatory multilingual names provided. Ontology anchors are approximate but defensible.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions (monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, trans) for this composite chicken katsu bowl dish are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for G00010540, and no sufficiently close composite match providing these fractions was found in USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg set to -1: full vitamin panel data for this composite dish is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for G00010540, and no sufficiently close composite match providing these values was found in USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: phosphorus, zinc, and selenium data for this composite dish are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for G00010540, and no sufficiently close composite match was found in USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite dish is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for G00010540 and was not available from USDA FoodData Central for a comparable composite as of 2026-04-16. Saturated fat (0.9 g/100g) estimated from USDA SR Legacy #05062 (chicken breast, breaded and fried) as the closest approved-source analogue; this value is documented in nutrient_source.source_match_notes. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on white pearl rice GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables; no direct GI measurement for chicken katsu bowl as a composite dish was found. Halal status marked false as preparation method (deep-frying, sauce ingredients) cannot be confirmed halal without restaurant-specific certification. The input record listed primary_cuisine as 'Western' which appears to be a data entry error; this record correctly classifies the dish as Japanese-Singaporean fusion based on its ingredients and preparation style.
